one_liner: Even Santa Claus can get cancer.
search_doc:
Even Santa Claus can get cancer.
Mechanisms: cultural-icon-subversion, seasonal-fundraising-hook, earned-broadcast-takeover, fictional-character-as-patient
Idea: A True Story is a 5-minute short film specially created by Gustave Roussy for the most crucial time for donations in France: Christmas. It tells the unexpected story of Santa Claus facing and surviving cancer.
Insight: If cancer can strike even the most universal, untouchable figure of Christmas — Santa Claus — donors can no longer treat cancer as someone else's problem.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PR/SILVER/A True Story - Gustave Roussy (Publicis Conseil, Paris).webp

extracted.tagline: The untold story of Santa Claus defeating cancer
extracted.context: Very few of us are aware of it, but we have never been closer to curing cancer. Today, 66% of cancers can be cured, compared to 40% a decade ago. Gustave Roussy, the leading French cancer institute, is at the forefront of these progress against cancer. However, these advancements only rely on donations. Problem: Due to economic inflation and the lack of media coverage, donations dramatically declined in France in 2023.
extracted.idea: A True Story is a 5-minute short film specially created by Gustave Roussy for the most crucial time for donations in France: Christmas. It tells the unexpected story of Santa Claus facing and surviving cancer.
execution: 5-minute short film released for the Christmas donation window
Coverage across French national TV channels (TF1, Brut, RTL, France TV, LCI, C8, NRJ12, W9, 6ter, TMC, CNews, France 24, MTV, RMC Info)
Press features in Le Parisien and BFM TV
Distribution via multiple media partners
metrics: +5.3 million euros in only two weeks
+14% in donations compared to last year

inferred.insight: If cancer can strike even the most universal, untouchable figure of Christmas — Santa Claus — donors can no longer treat cancer as someone else's problem.
inferred.one_liner: Even Santa Claus can get cancer.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reversed a national decline in donations during the critical Christmas window, raising 5.3M euros in two weeks (45% of the institute's annual donation total) and sustaining a 10% lift into January 2024.

visual.style_description: Cinematic, dimly-lit close-up portrait of an older Santa Claus character without his hat, gaunt and bald to suggest chemotherapy; the title 'A True Story' is set in a vintage serif over the portrait, flanked by press logos at the top and a column of yellow metric blocks on the right.

idea.rationale: Casting Santa Claus — the most universal Christmas icon — as a cancer patient is a single, instantly-legible cultural-icon subversion that earns broadcast attention on its own merits. The hook is tightly fitted to the Christmas donation window, but the device is essentially one sad-Santa short film rather than a new mechanism, which limits ceiling on originality.

strategy.rationale: Gustave Roussy is the leading French cancer institute and one of Europe's top oncology hospitals, funded heavily by individual donations, so a donation-collapse during the 2023 inflation period is a direct existential threat — not borrowed purpose. The board cites a credible data point — cure rates have risen from 40% to 66% over a decade — to argue that donations have material impact, which gives the ask substance. The insight that 'even Santa can get cancer' is emotionally on-brief for an oncology institute but rests on cultural shorthand rather than a verified behavioural-science source for why this specific reframe should unlock donor wallets.

execution.rationale: The 5-minute short film was rolled into the Christmas donation window across an unusually broad set of French national broadcasters (TF1, France TV, BFM TV, RTL, MTV, CNews, LCI and others), which is the right channel mix for a domestic French donor base. Phasing — a single film distributed via media partners — is conventional film-PR orchestration rather than a multi-stage earned engine, and there's no visible amplification layer (partnerships, celebrity, retail tie-in) beyond press distribution.

impact.rationale: The headline outcome is unusually strong for a donation campaign and given in absolute euros: +5.3M euros raised in two weeks, equivalent to 45% of Gustave Roussy's annual donation total — a real business outcome with an anchored base. The +14% year-on-year and +10% January-2024 lifts are stated as deltas but the absolute baselines aren't disclosed, so 'of what?' applies. The 18M earned impressions and 143.7M reach are entrant-reported and unaudited, but the broadcast list is credible and the financial outcome is the load-bearing proof, not the reach numbers.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the Santa-with-cancer subversion delivered an unusually strong donation outcome (45% of annual total in two weeks) but the campaign mechanism is single-asset broadcast PR rather than a category-defining earned engine.

Idea: Gen Z don't want to work in bars anymore. They want to work for companies and build a career. Heineken is one of the most attractive employers in Europe and UK. For the past 3 years […] Top Employer. So Heineken changed their hiring policy and named Bar Experience as a vital skill in the hiring process across the entire Heineken Company. execution: Bar backdoors were used for the first time as media as they turned into Heineken offices frontdoors
Heineken top management as influencers on social and […] platform with all the bars looking for staff where people could apply for jobs
After at least 3 months of bar work, they'd receive the Bar Experience skill on LinkedIn, that allowed Heineken to filter out candidates for openings of the Heineken Company
metrics: +5K bar jobs applications in the first two weeks

inferred.one_liner: Bar work becomes a vital skill on the Heineken CV.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Heineken rewired its global hiring policy into an industry-wide rescue for bar staffing, generating 5,000+ bar job applications in two weeks and prompting the Dutch government to create a fund to support bars.

visual.style_description: Cinematic portrait of a young man behind a Heineken-stocked bar at night, lit by warm bokeh, holding centre stage with the campaign title in serif typography to his left; press quote stickers float at the top, dense Italian body copy and a phone-mockup strip line the right column.

idea.rationale: Naming bar work as a vital skill in the Heineken hiring policy and back-dooring bar entrances as the brand's own office front doors is a sharp, native-PR reframe: a corporate HR document becomes a category rescue. The asset on the board reads instantly — a working bartender as the brand's next hire — and the LinkedIn 'Bar Experience' credential extends the idea past the launch window into a recurring earned moment.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is Heineken N.V.'s flagship lager and the largest international beer brand reliant on the European HORECA (hotels-restaurants-cafés) channel, so a post-pandemic staffing collapse in bars is a direct threat to its own distribution, not borrowed purpose. The insight that Gen Z prefer corporate careers over bar shifts is anchored by a stated 100,000 part-timers quitting hospitality since 2021 and by Heineken's Top Employer status, both of which give the policy change credible weight. The bill it pays — using its employer brand as the bait to refill bars — is exact mission-fit for a brewer whose volume depends on the on-trade.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs from a literal media reframe (bar back doors restaged as Heineken office front doors) into an executive-as-influencer social layer and a job-board platform connecting bars to applicants, closed off by a LinkedIn skill that filters Heineken's own pipeline. The phasing — policy announcement, social amplification by top management, three-month bar tenure, credential drop — keeps the campaign live for months rather than days, and the HORECA-adjacent press pickup (De Volkskrant, Het Parool, The Irish Times) targets the markets where the staffing crisis actually bites.

impact.rationale: The headline business outcome — +5,000 bar job applications in the first two weeks — is the right metric for a staffing campaign and is given as an absolute number rather than a percentage, which is a real RIGOUR win. '90% of our target reached' is a softer claim: 90% of what target audience, measured how, is not disclosed on the board. The qualitative footnote that the Dutch government created a fund to support bars is a credible policy-shift signal of cultural reach, though it's described rather than evidenced with a date or instrument.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because Heineken used its own hiring policy as the campaign — converting bar shifts into a LinkedIn credential is exact mission-fit for a brewer whose volume depends on bars staying open.

The victim's voicemail becomes the petition.
Mechanisms: victim-as-author, everyday-object-transformation, telecom-partnership-as-news, legislative-advocacy, audio-petition-mechanic
Idea: Child Focus gave his old voicemail a new purpose. Together with Glenn's provider, Orange, we transformed his original voicemail into an audio petition for a better online protection of minors. We invited people to call Glenn's old number and sign by leaving their name after the tone.

Old voicemail repurposed as audio petition
Public invited to call and leave their name as signature
Partnership with telecom provider Orange
TV news, talk-show and political coverage

press_coverage: 'The campaign of Child Focus is a wake-up call for policymakers' — De Morgen
'Child Focus asks to call Glenn's real number to address a real problem' — VRT
'Child Focus uses victim's phone as a symbol to fight against exposing' — RTL Info

inferred.insight: The phone that ruined a sexting victim's life can become the most powerful petition device against the abuse that killed him.
inferred.one_liner: The victim's voicemail becomes the petition.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reactivating Glenn's real phone number turned each public call into a recorded signature for an audio petition, reaching 40M people and driving a new Belgian law against exposing/sexting abuse of minors.

visual.style_description: Centre hero of a smartphone lockscreen showing a smiling photo of Glenn and '37,758 missed calls' against a dark teal velvet backdrop, with the campaign title and number 'CALL GLENN +32 493 79 58 30' beneath; press logos line the very top, screenshotted social messages float around the phone like incoming texts, and a bottom strip of TV/talk-show stills documents earned coverage.

idea.rationale: Reactivating the actual phone of a real sexting-victim who took his own life — and turning his old voicemail into the petition itself — is emotionally heavy and memorable, with the device that ruined his life becoming the device that legislates against it. The mechanic (call a real number, leave your name after the tone to sign) is unusually tactile for a petition.

strategy.rationale: Child Focus is Belgium's national missing-and-exploited-children foundation, and online minor-protection sits exactly inside its remit, so the work is on-mission rather than borrowed purpose. The context — 1 in 4 children are victims of 'exposing' (non-consensual sharing of intimate imagery) — is a real category problem; the board states it as a flat figure without a study citation, which is the weak spot. The use of Glenn's real identity is the strategic choice that gives the campaign moral weight but also depends entirely on the family's authorisation, which the board confirms by implication.

execution.rationale: The orchestration pairs Glenn's reactivated number (a credible earned-media artifact in its own right) with telecom-provider Orange as the technical partner, then layers TV news, talk-show and political coverage to push the audio-petition into parliamentary debate. Channel-fit is right for a Belgian legislative campaign — Flemish and Walloon outlets (VRT, RTL Info, De Standaard, Le Soir, De Morgen) cover both linguistic markets that any Belgian law needs.

impact.rationale: The strongest claim — that the campaign resulted in a new Belgian law against exposing — is the real legislative outcome a child-protection NGO would target, though the board does not name the law or cite its parliamentary reference, so the policy claim is asserted rather than evidenced. The 40 million people reached and €1.1M earned-media value are entrant-reported and large relative to Belgium's ~11.7M population, suggesting cross-border counting. As a Bronze-tier earned-media case the absolute base of 'reach' matters more than usual here.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the voicemail-as-petition mechanic is emotionally powerful and on-mission, but the law-passed claim is named without citation and the reach figure outruns Belgium's population without an obvious base.

visual.style_description: Centred CGI hero showing two photo-real Black women with distinct natural and braided hairstyles, flanked by a fan of smaller character renders that step gradually from amateur to brand-grade; clean off-white background, partner press logos top row, slim text columns left and metrics column right with branded colour accents.

idea.rationale: Treating Black hair representation as an open-source engineering problem — and publishing a 200+ page free reference library for game developers — converts Dove's beauty-equity argument into an industry tool rather than another awareness film. It is more memorable as a category move (a beauty brand writing a coder's manual) than as a single image, which is its mild limitation as a PR hook.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's mass beauty brand whose 'Real Beauty' platform has anchored representation work for two decades, so extending it from photography into video-game rendering is direct mission continuity, not borrowed purpose. The cited insight — that 85% of Black gamers feel Black hair is poorly represented in video games — is the right data point, though sourced to Dove's own research and not externally audited. Partnering with the Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists gives the work cultural authorship rather than a brand speaking on the community's behalf.

execution.rationale: The deliverable itself — a free, downloadable, technically usable guide with 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials and working files — is the campaign, with active engagement of major studios as the distribution layer. The Sarah Bond (President of Xbox) endorsement is the load-bearing earned-media moment that signals industry adoption, and coverage spans gaming press (gamesindustry.biz, Creative Bloq) and culture press (Rolling Stone, Vogue Business, Forbes), which is appropriate channel-fit for an industry-tool story.

impact.rationale: The 1.81B+ earned impressions figure is entrant-reported and unaudited, and the headline representation metrics — '85% of Black gamers now feel better represented' and '95% see the positive societal impact' — are percentages without an absolute base disclosed on the board, so 'of how many surveyed?' remains open. The '+32% brand loyalty amongst Black community' is the closest stand-in for a business outcome but is again unanchored. The Xbox-presidential endorsement and the existence of a working open-source asset are stronger structural proof than the percentages themselves.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the open-source toolkit is a genuine on-mission extension of Real Beauty into a new category, but the impact case rests on unbased representation percentages rather than measurable industry adoption.

one_liner: DoorDash delivers every other Super Bowl ad to one person.
search_doc:
DoorDash delivers every other Super Bowl ad to one person.
Mechanisms: cultural-event-hijack, competitor-as-co-creator, promo-code-as-stunt, real-time-newsmaking
Idea: Every time another brand's Super Bowl ad debuted, we added their products into our online cart, so the world could keep track in real-time. Then after four straight quarters of brand, influencer, and internet engagement during the game, our own Super Bowl ad aired, featuring an incredibly long promo code that you had to enter correctly for a chance to get everything advertised.
Insight: DoorDash was renowned for restaurant delivery, but not for the millions of other products now offered on the app. So during the Super Bowl, when the other brands ran their own ads, DoorDash saw an opportunity to prove that it could now deliver pretty much anything-by delivering all the products, from all the other Super Bowl ads, all to one person.

extracted.tagline: Delivering Everything Advertised From Every Super Bowl Commercial
extracted.insight: DoorDash was renowned for restaurant delivery, but not for the millions of other products now offered on the app. So during the Super Bowl, when the other brands ran their own ads, DoorDash saw an opportunity to prove that it could now deliver pretty much anything-by delivering all the products, from all the other Super Bowl ads, all to one person.
extracted.idea: Every time another brand's Super Bowl ad debuted, we added their products into our online cart, so the world could keep track in real-time. Then after four straight quarters of brand, influencer, and internet engagement during the game, our own Super Bowl ad aired, featuring an incredibly long promo code that you had to enter correctly for a chance to get everything advertised.
press_coverage: NBC - Caught The Internet By Storm
ABC News - DoorDash Crushed The Super Bowl

inferred.context: DoorDash is known for restaurant delivery but now sells almost any product on its app — and needed a Super Bowl moment to prove it.
inferred.one_liner: DoorDash delivers every other Super Bowl ad to one person.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned every rival Super Bowl spot into DoorDash inventory and dared America to copy a single absurdly long promo code, generating 11.9B impressions, 8M entries, and earned credit as the night's commercial winner.

visual.style_description: CGI hero of a giant DoorDash bag overflowing with Super Bowl-advertised products on a stadium-orange sunset backdrop, headline arched above; press-quote pull-outs flank the bag, with screen-grab thumbnails and a three-column insight/idea/results block running across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Hijacking every other brand's Super Bowl ad by adding their products into a DoorDash cart in real time — then daring one viewer to type an impossibly long promo code for the whole haul — is a sharp piece of category-defining mischief. The mechanic turns competitors' paid media into DoorDash inventory and converts the night's biggest paid event into a single earned-media stunt, though the 'hijack the Super Bowl' format is a well-trodden PR genre.

strategy.rationale: DoorDash is the US category-leading last-mile delivery platform whose strategic problem is that consumers still slot it as 'restaurants only' even though the app now sells nearly any retail product. Demonstrating range by physically delivering items from competitors' Super Bowl ads is precise mission-fit: the brand needed proof of breadth and the Super Bowl provided 76 ready-made product demos at scale. The insight (DoorDash perceived as food-only) is stated rather than data-backed on the board, leaving the strategic gap self-asserted.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs across all four quarters of the live broadcast: each rival ad triggers a real-time cart update, social plays scoreboard during the game, then DoorDash's own spot lands the promo-code mechanic as the closing payoff. Securing 76 official and unofficial partnerships across rival advertisers, plus actually fulfilling 2,468 individual product deliveries, turned a stunt concept into a working logistics demonstration.

impact.rationale: 11.9B impressions and 8M promo-code submissions are headline reach figures that are entrant-reported and aggregate-only — 'impressions' for a Super Bowl-adjacent stunt are notoriously inflated, and the board offers no audited equivalent. The 8M submissions is a credible engagement proxy (it required active typing), but the actual business outcome for DoorDash — new-category orders, non-food cart growth, retention — is conspicuously absent. Press credit from NBC, TIME and ABC reads as enthusiasm rather than independently measured commercial uplift.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the real-time competitor-ad hijack is genuinely original and on-brief for proving DoorDash sells beyond food, but the impact case leans on impressions and submissions rather than on the non-food order growth the campaign was built to drive.

one_liner: Taco Bell sues to give 'Taco Tuesday' back to everyone.
search_doc:
Taco Bell sues to give 'Taco Tuesday' back to everyone.
Mechanisms: legal-filing-as-campaign, category-leadership-stunt, celebrity-endorsement-as-cause, petition-driven-mobilisation
Idea: As the leading brand in Taco Culture, we knew we had to tackle the whole 'Taco Tuesday' trademark situation head-on. So, instead of just running a typical ad campaign, we decided to take some real action. Like, legal action.
Insight: Trademark law is invisible to consumers, but freeing a common food phrase is a fight everyone instinctively roots for.

extracted.tagline: A legal filing becomes an ad becomes a campaign becomes an ongoing brand platform.
extracted.context: In the United States, the phrase 'Taco Tuesday' is trademarked, preventing restaurants, food trucks, and small vendors from using this common term on what should be one of their busiest days of the week. Unfortunately, this trademark enforcement has led to small taco vendors receiving cease-and-desist letters when attempting to promote their own 'Taco Tuesday' specials.

We partnered up with iconic 'lawyer TikTokers' to help break down the legal stuff and explain it in a way that everyone could understand
We also got our fans involved by rallying them to sign a petition on change.org. It was all about getting the community fired up and ready to support the cause
Finally, we enlisted Taco Tuesday's biggest fan, LeBron, was the perfect teammate because we didn't just want any celebrity partner. We needed someone who believed in the cause as much as we did

inferred.insight: Trademark law is invisible to consumers, but freeing a common food phrase is a fight everyone instinctively roots for.
inferred.one_liner: Taco Bell sues to give 'Taco Tuesday' back to everyone.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A trademark petition turned into a 21B-impression brand platform: 100,000+ small taco vendors gained the right to use 'Taco Tuesday' and Taco Bell positioned itself as the category's defender rather than its monopolist.

visual.style_description: Photo of LeBron James in a yellow tee holding a taco against a sunlit landscape, with a faux-rubber-stamped legal petition (Live Más logo and 'FREEING TACO T***DAY' text) overlapping the centre as the hero artefact; purple band underneath holds five large press pull-quotes (Forbes, The Guardian, FOX, Takeout, USA Today), bottom strip shows three small body-copy blocks above a wide row of press logos (NYT, CNN, WSJ, Mashable, Reuters, etc.).

idea.rationale: Filing a real US Patent & Trademark Office petition to liberate 'Taco Tuesday' from a competitor's trademark — and turning the legal filing itself into the ad — is a clean legal-document-as-creative-asset move. It is structurally similar to other recent 'brand sues rival on principle' PR plays (Heinz/Mayochup, etc.), so the device feels familiar even if the application to a beloved colloquial phrase is fresh.

strategy.rationale: Taco Bell is Yum! Brands' QSR Mexican-inspired category leader whose stated brand mission is 'Live Más' and championing taco culture, so attacking a trademark that prevents 100,000+ small taco vendors from using 'Taco Tuesday' is mission-coherent — the leader defends the category rather than monopolises it. The insight that consumers don't know about the trademark but instinctively support freeing a common phrase is plausible cultural reading rather than data-backed on the board. The play does carry an unspoken commercial logic (Taco Bell weakens a smaller competitor's defensive moat), which slightly complicates the 'pure altruism' framing.

execution.rationale: The orchestration layers a genuine legal filing with TikTok lawyer-explainers (translating the legal action for a lay audience), a change.org petition (giving the public a participation handle), and LeBron James as the celebrity authenticator who personally championed Taco Tuesday already. The phases follow a classic PR-loop arc — file, explain, mobilise, endorse — and use channels suited to each layer's job. The press tier (NYT, Guardian, CNN, WSJ, Reuters) shows the legal story did earn beyond ad-trade.

impact.rationale: 100,000+ businesses gaining the right to promote 'Taco Tuesday' is the right cultural-outcome metric and is the concrete legal win (the USPTO did cancel the trademark in the relevant case), giving the campaign a real policy result. The 21B impressions and 11,000+ feature stories are entrant-reported aggregates without an audited base, and the 407% lift in social mentions of 'Taco Tuesday with Taco Bell' is an association metric, not a sales or footfall outcome. Taco Bell's actual business outcome — visit frequency on Tuesdays, taco unit sales — is conspicuously absent from the proof case.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because filing a real USPTO petition is a fresh PR mechanism and a real legal win for 100,000+ small vendors, but the case rests on impressions and association lift rather than on Taco Bell's own business outcome, and the legal-filing-as-PR device is no longer category-new.

But for a PR submission the execution lacks every native PR mechanic: no press launch, no partnership reveal moment, no cultural-conversation orchestration, no influencer or media-relations layer, no phasing. The native-to-category hard cap applies because what is shown is brand-identity craft, not earned-media execution.

impact.rationale: The board reports zero quantitative metrics and lists zero press pickups, so the real business outcome a tilapia producer would care about — sales, distribution lift, price-premium realisation, export-market entry — is conspicuously absent (RIGOUR pattern 3). The qualitative claim that the haloed fish 'became a national icon' is an entrant assertion with no audited reach, share-of-voice or sentiment data behind it (RIGOUR pattern 2). With neither business proof nor earned-coverage proof on the board, the Impact case rests entirely on the existence of the identity system itself.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the folk-legend-as-brand-of-origin is locally distinctive and the design craft is high, but the work behaves as brand identity submitted into PR — no earned-media engine, no metrics, no press pickup — which caps it just inside the bottom of the metal.

How could we get this all over the internet, when our creativity had no presence online? We placed actual PR, social, and influencer plays with publications from Daily Mail to New York Times. Partnering with meme accounts, podcasters, and influencers, [...] you name it. The more Michael was used than [...] the more people were invested. We got out of the internet[…]. Then at the moment, anyone, People didn't just watch, they participated in a conspiracy about a skincare brand! Michael CeraVe was everywhere, it became a game hot topic debate which led to 11 Billion impressions BEFORE the commercial [aired]. We reused the truth during the most over watched Super Bowl. Our commercial wasn't the hero like most Super Bowl spots: it was the resolution. Of a month-long, fan-driven, agency-backed Comedy legend. The 'A-list' directed a massive intrigue, fan-driven dream, which was hailed as 'Perfect.' [TODAY], to the rest, dermatologists reject Michael making it clear shot and for all, CeraVe is developed with dermatologists. We Michael Cera'd everyone.

idea.rationale: Building a month-long pre-Super-Bowl conspiracy that Michael Cera secretly invented CeraVe — leveraging the homophone hidden inside his own name — is a memorable and structurally clever inversion of the standard Super Bowl playbook, where the reveal is the punchline rather than the asset. The idea's originality lies less in the celebrity choice than in choosing to spend a month pretending the brand isn't running a Super Bowl ad at all.

strategy.rationale: CeraVe is L'Oréal's mass dermatologist-developed moisturiser brand whose strategic moat is clinical credibility, and the stated goal was to make 'developed with dermatologists' famous at the Super Bowl. The conspiracy mechanic actively obscures that proof point for a month — the joke lands but the strategic message (it's the dermatologists, not the actor) only arrives in the final spot, and the jury may have read this as memorability bought at the cost of message clarity. The insight is implicit rather than data-backed; the conspiracy's success depended on a pre-existing internet appetite for Michael Cera lore that the board does not size.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is the strongest element: a month of cryptic teaser clips, meme-account partnerships, podcaster placements and tier-1 press hits from Daily Mail to The New York Times, building a fan-driven conspiracy that culminates in a Super Bowl reveal designed to function as resolution rather than launch. Channel mix is appropriate for a US-internet-native gag and the SNL/Jimmy Fallon late-night pickups are the load-bearing cultural-validation moments.

impact.rationale: The headline 30 Billion earned impressions and 15B pre-Super-Bowl impressions are entrant-reported and unaudited, and 30B is at the upper extreme of plausibility for a single brand campaign, which weakens rather than strengthens the proof. why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the conspiracy is genuinely original and the cultural traction is real, but the strategic dermatologist message gets crowded out by the joke and every headline metric on the board is a percentage or impression count without an absolute base.

Channel-fit is right for a legislative-advocacy push, though some metric lines on the board ('1.2 Billion media outlets', '+1.6 Billion campaign emotional, native') read as garbled or duplicated.

The '22% increase in gun reform conversation' is a percentage without an absolute base or measurement window disclosed. why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the AI-voice mechanic is original and genuinely on-mission, but for a campaign whose stated objective is moving gun legislation, the board can prove calls placed and press won without proving any law shifted.

Nosotras Women Connecting, an NGO that promotes women's rights, proposed a bill that aimed to provide these products for free in schools. We had to convince the Costa Rican congress to approve a law that wasn't even on their radar.
Insight: Politicians can ignore a bill, but they can't ignore being personally added to a WhatsApp group where girls thank them by name for a law they haven't passed yet.

Voice-note and video messages sent in the chat by Costa Rican girls thanking them, naming them by name and in advance, for approving a law that would forever change their future

inferred.context: Costa Rican girls miss school during their periods because they cannot afford menstrual products, but a free-products bill sat untouched on the congressional agenda.
inferred.insight: Politicians can ignore a bill, but they can't ignore being personally added to a WhatsApp group where girls thank them by name for a law they haven't passed yet.
inferred.one_liner: The girls add congress to one inescapable WhatsApp group.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Five days of WhatsApp voice notes from Costa Rican schoolgirls to every congressmember turned a dormant bill into a unanimously approved Menstrual Justice Law, a concrete legislative outcome reported by CNN.

visual.style_description: Composite centre hero of a smartphone WhatsApp 'Gracias Diputados' group chat dropped over a dark, half-empty congress chamber; left column carries the campaign headline and idea copy in white serif, right column has four short labelled blocks (Finding / Idea / outcome) plus a CNN press quote, with thumbnail screenshots of girls' video messages running down the left side.

idea.rationale: Adding every Costa Rican congressmember to a single WhatsApp group where schoolgirls thank them by name for a law they haven't yet passed is a sharp platform-jailbreak that weaponises pre-thanking — the device is its own news event. The mechanic is precisely scaled to a small national parliament where every name matters, which is part of what makes it work.

strategy.rationale: Nosotras Women Connecting (Cambiemos La Regla) is a Costa Rican NGO advocating women's rights, so authoring a menstrual-justice bill and lobbying congress to pass it is direct mission-fit, not borrowed purpose for a brand. The insight — that Costa Rican girls miss school during their periods because they cannot afford menstrual products — is a real, locally-specific equity gap, though the board does not cite a specific study or a national-statistics figure for how many girls are affected. The strategic angle (legislators ignore bills; they can't ignore being personally tagged by their constituents) is a credible reading of small-parliament political behaviour.

execution.rationale: Orchestration ran over five days as a sustained WhatsApp campaign of voice notes and videos from the affected girls themselves — a victim-as-author cascade rather than the NGO speaking on their behalf — and the press hook is the CNN report on the unanimous passage of the law, which closes the loop on the lobbying objective. The channel choice (WhatsApp group chat) is highly relevant to the target (politicians who keep their phones on for constituents), but the board shows only one external earned moment (CNN) rather than a fuller multi-press cascade, and the craft of the board itself is rated medium.

impact.rationale: The headline outcome is the strongest possible business proof for a legislative-advocacy campaign: the Menstrual Justice Law was unanimously approved by the Costa Rican congress, with CNN as a credible external verifier. That is a real legal-system change, not a surrogate metric. The metrics array is otherwise empty — no reach, sentiment, or schoolgirl-attendance figures are disclosed — so the case rests entirely on the legislative win, which is the right hierarchy but leaves the cultural-reach dimension unmeasured.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the WhatsApp pre-thanking mechanic delivered the rare ultimate PR outcome — a unanimously approved national law — but the board's broader impact and reach evidence beyond CNN is thin.

inferred.qualitative_summary: An AI-aged version of a 9-year-old girl confronted parents worldwide with the long-term cost of oversharing, reaching 3.7B impressions across 70+ countries and shifting two-thirds of parents to commit to sharing with care.

visual.style_description: Close-up of a young girl's face glitched and torn by digital scan-lines as if her image is being deepfaked, set on a dark background; left column holds the challenge/idea/results text, right column is a wall of telecom partner logos, press logos, metric callouts and social mock-ups including a QR-coded SharkWithCare filter.

idea.rationale: Using AI to project a 9-year-old into her own future and have her confront her parents for sharing her photos is a structurally clever inversion: the same technology that creates the harm (deepfakes built on shared images) becomes the technology that warns about it. The 'aged-up child speaks back from the future' device is emotionally singular and visually arresting enough to carry a PR cycle, though the 'future-self warns present-self' narrative trope itself is not new.

strategy.rationale: Deutsche Telekom is Europe's largest telecoms operator (T-Mobile parent), and its long-running ShareWithCare programme positions the brand as Europe's caretaker of digital well-being, so a campaign about children's data privacy is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The '75% of parents share their kids' data on social media without their consent' insight is cited as the campaign's foundation but the source isn't named on the board, so the load-bearing statistic remains entrant-asserted. The deepfake-on-stolen-childhood-photos risk is plausible and topical, anchoring the wake-up call in a real emerging harm.

execution.rationale: The orchestration pairs a single hero film (the AI-aged Ella) with a working product layer — a social filter that pixelates children's faces — and a hashtag (#ShareWithCare) so the campaign moves from horror to behaviour change inside one journey. Telecom partner logos on the board signal a coalition rollout across Deutsche Telekom's European footprint, and the press-tier targeting (CNN, NYT, GMA, Forbes, Le Monde, ELLE) shows deliberate parenting and tech-news placement rather than ad-trade press alone.

impact.rationale: The '2 out of 3 parents oppose sharing their kids online and will instead #ShareWithCare in the future' figure is the right behaviour-change outcome for a digital-literacy campaign, though it reads as survey-stated intent rather than observed behaviour shift. The 3.7B impressions and 51M media-outlet reach are entrant-reported aggregates ('3.7B of what audited count?'), while the 400+ outlets and named placements (CNN, NYT, Forbes, Le Monde) are a credible breadth signal of earned conversation. No telecom-business outcome — sign-ups, ARPU, brand-preference shift — is shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because an AI-aged child confronting her parents fuses an emerging cultural fear (deepfakes from oversharing) with Deutsche Telekom's stated digital-well-being mission, carried by tier-one global press into a survey-measured shift in parental intent.

one_liner: Women's football, disguised as men's football.
search_doc:
Women's football, disguised as men's football.
Mechanisms: deepfake-bias-reveal, cultural-symbol-subversion, audience-trap-and-reveal, purpose-driven-campaign
Idea: We created a compilation of unseen highlights of the French men's football team, showcasing powerful shots, amazing dribbles… only sensational actions. But the viewer didn't know he was actually enjoying women's football: we swapped the women's French team players with the men's starts from the French team, to make the audience watch their performances with fresh eyes.
Insight: To combat the gender bias, we decided to show how exciting women's football actually is.

extracted.tagline: If you love football, you love women's football.
extracted.context: In July 2023, the French women's football team was about to participate in its 5th World Cup. But, a few weeks before the competition, no media organization had secured the broadcasting rights for the event. All this due to women bashing: in a country passionate about football, women's football is still seen as a lesser form of football, less technical, physical and entertaining.
extracted.insight: To combat the gender bias, we decided to show how exciting women's football actually is.
extracted.idea: We created a compilation of unseen highlights of the French men's football team, showcasing powerful shots, amazing dribbles… only sensational actions. But the viewer didn't know he was actually enjoying women's football: we swapped the women's French team players with the men's starts from the French team, to make the audience watch their performances with fresh eyes.
execution: Campaign first unfolded on Twitter, the most engaging platform for football fans
An influencer posted the content in two parts: he first posted the so-called unseen sensational highlights that excited football fans
Few hours later, he revealed the trick by releasing the full video, exposing the viewers to their own gender bias
Successful press quickly gained media attention in France and worldwide within a day of the initial tweet
press_coverage: "Women's soccer can be just as exciting as men's" — TIME

inferred.one_liner: Women's football, disguised as men's football.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used VFX to trick football fans into cheering women's plays they assumed were men's, then revealed the swap — earning 2 billion impressions and press in 91 countries that reframed perceptions of the women's game ahead of the 2023 World Cup.

visual.style_description: Stadium-night hero composition with the French women's national team in blue jerseys arranged in a triangle below an orange-accented 'WoMen's Football' wordmark and the headline 'IF YOU LOVE FOOTBALL, YOU LOVE WOMEN'S FOOTBALL'; left column is dense body copy in sections (Insight, Concept, Orchestration), right column stacks four large orange metric callouts, bottom strip shows five film stills from the reveal video and the Orange logo block.

idea.rationale: VFX-swapping the faces of the French men's national team onto real women's-team plays — so fans cheer the action believing it is men's football, then discover their own bias — is a single-mechanic reveal that perfectly weaponises the audience against itself. It is provocative, repeatable beyond a single match, and impossible to debate once viewers have reacted before the reveal.

strategy.rationale: Orange is France's incumbent telecoms operator and a long-standing sponsor of the Fédération Française de Football (men's and women's teams), so championing the women's game on the eve of the 2023 World Cup is direct sponsor-territory advocacy rather than borrowed purpose. The board grounds the cultural premise in a hard, verifiable fact — no French broadcaster had secured the 2023 Women's World Cup rights weeks before kick-off — which is the strongest possible piece of insight veracity for the 'women's football is under-valued' thesis. The work also addresses an audience the brand demonstrably needs (football-passionate French households) rather than a generic gender-equality message.

execution.rationale: Launching first on Twitter — then the highest-engagement platform for French football fandom — via a credible football influencer in a deliberate two-part post (highlights first, reveal hours later) is exactly the sequence needed to trap then expose the bias, and the structure made the reveal itself the news. Earned press picked it up in 91 countries within a day, which is the right amplification path for a film-led PR moment riding a fixed-date sports calendar.

impact.rationale: The business outcome a telco sponsor cares about — share of voice and reputational lift around the women's World Cup — is well-served by the 91-country coverage and named press from TIME and ESPN, though those press logos are entrant-reported and the campaign does not show a downstream broadcast-rights or viewership-lift figure that would close the loop on the original problem. '200M organic views' and '2B impressions' are unbased platform-side claims with no engagement-rate or completion data, but '50M likes' is a directionally credible engagement signal for a single video on Twitter.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because the bias-reveal mechanic makes the audience indict itself — and Orange, as the actual sponsor of French football, had the standing to deliver it on the eve of a World Cup that France was about to play without a broadcaster.
